front: Play: garbage collector and former baseball player who could not break into the MLB because of the color barrier
Play: protagonist prevents his son Cory from accepting a football scholarship, forcing him to work at A&P
Play: brain-damaged WWII veteran with a metal plate in his head believes he is an archangel and blows a broken trumpet to open the gates of Heaven at his brother's funeral
Play: Gabriel's dancing at his brother Troy's funeral is described as 'slow, strange' and 'atavistic'
Play: protagonist's affair with Alberta produces the daughter Raynell, whom Rose adopts after Alberta dies in childbirth
Play: protagonist refers to death as 'nothing but a fastball on the outside corner' and taunts Death while building the title object
Play: protagonist wrestled with Death for 'three days and three nights'
Play: protagonist warns his son Cory that he has committed 'strike two' and is 'living with a full count' before chasing him out of the house with a baseball bat
Play: characters sing a song about the possum-treeing dog Blue who 'treed a possum in Noah's Ark'
Play: Bono bets buying his wife a refrigerator on the protagonist finishing the title object
Play: Rose laments 'I planted myself inside you and waited to bloom' to her unfaithful husband
Play: the protagonist tells Rose that he 'planted a seed' but 'the soil was hard and rocky'
Play: James Earl Jones originated the role of Troy Maxson on Broadway
Play: 2016 film adaptation directed by Denzel Washington, who has pledged to produce films of all ten Pittsburgh Cycle plays
Play: Boy Willie wants to sell the title object to buy Sutter's land, but his sister Berniece refuses
Play: the family's history was carved into the title object by the slave carpenter Willie Boy
Play: Berniece uses the title object to exorcise the ghost of the slave owner Sutter
Play: four characters — Boy Charles and hobos burned alive in a boxcar by a lynch mob — become the Ghosts of the Yellow Dog
Play: the Ghosts of the Yellow Dog are blamed for Sutter falling down a well
Play: one character sings 'I want you to help me' eighteen times, only stopping to invoke family members' names, to exorcise a ghost
Play: the washed-up musician Wining Boy sells his silk 'magic suit' and shoes to Lymon, promising amatory effects
Play: Wining Boy recounts 'It felt like the longer I stood there the bigger I got' after calling out to the Ghosts of the Yellow Dog at a railway crossing
Play: Boy Willie and Lymon arrive from Mississippi in a truck full of watermelons
Play: the uncle Doaker manages the household and tells the story of how the title object was carved
Play: Reverend Avery courts Berniece, who teaches her daughter Maretha to play the title instrument
Play: working title Mill Hand's Lunch Bucket was inspired by a Romare Bearden painting
Play: Samuel L. Jackson served as the male lead (Boy Willie) at its first live performance
Play: the only Pittsburgh Cycle entry not set in Pittsburgh — it takes place in a Chicago recording studio
Play: a blues singer refuses to start a recording session without a Coca-Cola
Play: the trumpeter Levee stabs the pianist Toledo for stepping on his treasured Florsheim shoes
Play: stuttering Sylvester mars the intro to a recording session
Play: 2020 Netflix film adaptation starring Viola Davis and Chadwick Boseman
Play: Herald Loomis searches for his wife Martha Pentecost after being captured and enslaved by the title character for seven years
back: Joe Turner's Come and Gone (August Wilson)

front: Play: a man slashes his chest and rubs blood on his face, finding his 'song of self-sufficiency'
back: Joe Turner's Come and Gone (August Wilson)

front: Play: the conjure man Bynum sings a song about the man who captured and enslaved Herald Loomis
back: Joe Turner's Come and Gone (August Wilson)

front: Play: Bynum describes following a 'shiny man' whom the 'People Finder' Selig is hired to find
back: Joe Turner's Come and Gone (August Wilson)

front: Play: set at the craftsman Seth Holly's boarding house; the children Reuben and Zonia leave to look at some pigeons
back: Joe Turner's Come and Gone (August Wilson)

front: Playwright: author of the ten-play Pittsburgh Cycle (Century Cycle) depicting Black life in each decade of the 20th century, set in the Hill District of Pittsburgh
Playwright: recurring clairvoyant 'washer of souls' Aunt Ester lives at 1839 Wylie Avenue and ages from 285 to 366 across the cycle
Play: Risa, a follower of Prophet Samuel, slashes her legs with a razor to avoid being treated as a sex object
Play: Sterling smashes a butcher's window to steal a ham for his deceased friend Hambone's coffin
Play: Sterling, Wolf, and Holloway are patrons of Memphis Lee's diner
Play: Booster takes over Jim Becker's unofficial taxi cab business after being released from jail for killing a woman
Play: Youngblood saves up to buy Rena a house as a surprise
Play: Floyd 'Schoolboy' Barton achieves posthumous success after King Hedley kills him, mistaking him for the musician Buddy Bolden delivering money
Play: an ex-con who is the second of his name sells stolen refrigerators to try to buy a video store
Play: the protagonist is advised by the prophetic wise man Stool Pigeon and eventually killed by his own mother Ruby
Play: the title character cuts the protagonist's throat and quotes 'Jesus Christ was a big man. He was the Son of the Father. I too. I am the son of my father'
Play: Aunt Ester dies at age 366 in this play
Play: Solly Two Kings burns down a steel mill and is shot by the policeman Caesar Wilks
Play: Citizen Barlow comes to the 285-year-old Aunt Ester at 1839 Wylie Avenue to be cleansed
Playwright: cited the 'four B's' — Jorge Luis Borges, Amiri Baraka, Romare Bearden, and the blues — as his main influences
Playwright: many of his plays feature what he called 'spectacle characters' — mentally disabled figures who provide spiritual wisdom
Playwright: slammed Robert Brustein in his speech/manifesto 'The Ground on Which I Stand'
